title: File I/O and NIO.2
learningObjectives: Explain File I/O and NIO.2 with clear interview-ready wording
Implement File I/O and NIO.2 using compilable Java examples for Day 17
Identify and prevent common bugs related to File I/O and NIO.2
Discuss design and performance trade-offs for File I/O and NIO.2
Solve at least one realistic exercise based on File I/O and NIO.2
Answer conceptual, coding, and scenario interview questions confidently

sections.title: Why File I/O and NIO.2 matters
sections.content:
File I/O and NIO.2 is a high-impact topic for Day 17. It directly supports OOP principles and core Java APIs and appears in interviews as a depth-check area.

Why this matters in practice:
- Teams rely on this concept for clean implementation decisions.
- Mistakes here create maintainability and production-risk issues.
- Strong understanding improves both coding rounds and system discussions.

Learning strategy for this day:
1. Define the concept clearly in one line.
2. Implement one minimal Java example.
3. Validate at least one edge/failure case.
4. Explain one trade-off (simplicity vs flexibility, speed vs safety, etc.).
5. Rephrase your answer as if explaining to an interviewer.

If you can explain File I/O and NIO.2 clearly and implement it without copy-paste, you are interview-ready for this unit.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - File I/O and NIO.2
sections.content:
### Day 17 Topic: File I/O and NIO.2
This lesson belongs to Phase 2 (OOP principles and core Java APIs) and targets Beginner level expectations.

### Core Concept
Start by defining File I/O and NIO.2 in precise terms, then connect it to runtime behavior and design impact.

### Internal Understanding
For this topic, interviewers expect more than API recall. They look for:
- correct mental model
- edge-case awareness
- clean implementation choices
- trade-off reasoning

### Practical Implementation Approach
1. Build a baseline solution that is correct and readable.
2. Add validation for boundary, null/empty, or failure scenarios.
3. Measure or reason about time/space or operational cost.
4. Refactor into maintainable units.

### Interview Structure (recommended)
- What is it?
- Why is it needed?
- One practical Java example
- One common pitfall
- One better alternative (if constraints change)

### Production Perspective
In real systems, correctness alone is not enough. File I/O and NIO.2 should also support observability, safe changes, and team readability.

### Revision Checklist
- Can you explain this without notes?
- Can you code it from memory?
- Can you test one edge case quickly?
- Can you justify your approach in 30-60 seconds?

### Satyverse — deeper path (theory → practice)
**`Path` + `Files` API (NIO.2)** is the modern default — `Paths.get`, `Files.readString`, `Files.lines` with try-with-resources.

**Charsets:** always specify `StandardCharsets.UTF_8` when converting bytes — platform default charset breaks on different servers.

**Buffered I/O:** wrap streams for large files; `InputStream` / `Reader` distinction — bytes vs characters.

**Serialization caveat:** Java serialization is brittle for long-lived APIs — prefer JSON/Protobuf for service boundaries; know `Serializable` for caches and legacy only.

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Read all lines from a file with Files.readString (Java 11+) — simple text ingestion.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Walk a directory tree with Files.walk — filter and count .java files.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Path API: resolve sibling paths safely without string concatenation.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 17
sections.description: Visual model for File I/O and NIO.2

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Treating File I/O and NIO.2 as memorization instead of implementation practice.
Skipping edge-case tests and validating only happy path.
Using unclear naming and tightly coupled method logic.
Giving interview answers without concrete examples.

sections.title: Exercise - Practice File I/O and NIO.2
sections.problem:
Implement a Java solution for **File I/O and NIO.2** with practical constraints.

Requirements:
1. Handle at least 5 test inputs.
2. Include one explicit edge/failure case.
3. Print deterministic output for verification.
4. Add short comments for decision points.
5. Keep the solution interview-readable and maintainable.
hints: Start with correctness first, optimize later.
Add one guard clause for invalid input.
Refactor method names before final answer.

// Day 17 File I/O and NIO.2 - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is java.io vs java.nio?
conceptual.answer: Classic streams vs NIO buffers/channels—NIO.2 (java.nio.file) for modern file APIs.

followUps.question: Interview follow-up: how does **File I/O and NIO.2** show up in **production** debugging or **on-call**?
followUps.answer: Tie **logs**, **metrics**, **repro** steps, and **ownership** to what you already said. **Interviewers** reward **symptom → cause → fix → verification**, not textbook **definitions** alone. Classic streams vs NIO buffers/channels—NIO.2 (java.nio.file) for modern file APIs. Name **one failure mode** that looks like a bug but is really a misunderstanding. Tie the answer to **interview structure**: clarify, answer, then **one** concrete example. Connect the idea to something you can **observe** in **logs**, **tests**, or a **local run**.

wrongAnswers: Files.readAllLines is always the best choice for any file size.
Reader reads raw bytes without decoding.
Path and File are unrelated—you cannot convert between them.
IOException is unchecked so you can ignore it.
Default charset is always UTF-8 on every JVM.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define File I/O and NIO.2 in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |
| Phase Context | Align with phase 2 focus | Show progression mindset | Isolated-topic thinking |
